{{Infobox scientist
| name = Paul Piff
| birth_name = Paul Kayhan Piff
| birth_date = {{birth year and age|1981}} <!-- approximate -->
| fields = [[Social psychology]]
| workplaces = [[University of California, Irvine]]
| alma_mater = [[Reed College]] (BA)<br>[[University of California, Berkeley]] (MA, PhD)
| doctoral_advisor = [[Dacher Keltner]]
| known_for = Research on social class and ethics, psychology of awe
| awards = SAGE Young Scholar Award (2019)<br>APS Rising Star (2016)<br>NSF Graduate Research Fellowship
}}

{{Short description|American Psychologist and Professor}}

'''Paul Kayhan Piff''' (born c. 1981) is an American [[social psychology|social psychologist]] and Associate Professor of Psychology at the [[University of California, Irvine]]. His research focuses on [[social class]], [[economic inequality]], moral behavior, [[prosocial behavior|prosocial tendencies]], and the social functions of emotions such as awe.<ref name="uci-faculty">{{cite web |title=Paul K. Piff |url=https://faculty.sites.uci.edu/piff/ |website=UCI Faculty Sites |publisher=University of California, Irvine |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2026-02-26 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260226113446/https://faculty.sites.uci.edu/piff/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="greater-good">{{cite web |title=Paul Piff |url=https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/paul_piff |website=Greater Good Science Center |publisher=University of California, Berkeley |access-date=2026-02-17}}</ref>

His work has been the subject of national media coverage, including a cover profile in [[New York (magazine)|''New York magazine'']].<ref name="nymag">{{cite news |last=Miller |first=Lisa |title=The Money-Empathy Gap |url=https://longreads.com/2012/07/02/the-money-empathy-gap/ |work=New York Magazine |date=July 1, 2012 |access-date=2026-02-17}}</ref> In 2013 he delivered a [[TEDx]] talk titled “Does Money Make You Mean?”, presenting research on inequality to a public audience. The talk has received several million views online.<ref name="ted-talk">{{cite web |title=Paul Piff: Does money make you mean? |url=https://www.ted.com/talks/paul_piff_does_money_make_you_mean |website=TED |date=December 2013 |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2026-02-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260209171113/https://www.ted.com/talks/paul_piff_does_money_make_you_mean |url-status=live }}</ref> He has also appeared in documentary films including [[Park Avenue: Money, Power and the American Dream|Park Avenue: Money, Power and the American Dream]] and [[Capital in the Twenty-First Century (film)|Capital in the Twenty-First Century]].<ref name="imdb">{{cite web |title=Paul Piff |url=https://www.imdb.com/name/nm5366990/ |website=IMDb |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2017-02-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170219205537/http://www.imdb.com/name/nm5366990/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

== Education and early career ==

Piff grew up in Seattle, Washington, before moving at age four with his family to [[Haifa]], [[Israel]], where his father worked as an archivist, and his mother was a translator for the [[Baha'i World Centre|Baha'i World Center]].<ref name="reed-mag">{{cite news |title=The Dynamics of Compassion |url=https://www.reed.edu/reed-magazine/articles/2017/piff-altruism-psychology.html |work=Reed Magazine |date=March 2017 |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2025-11-08 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251108035021/https://www.reed.edu/reed-magazine/articles/2017/piff-altruism-psychology.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

He received a [[Bachelor of Arts|B.A]] in psychology from [[Reed College]] in 2004.<ref name="reed-mag" /> He earned his M.A. (2008) and Ph.D. (2012) in psychology from the [[University of California, Berkeley]], where he studied under [[Dacher Keltner]]. His doctoral dissertation, received the First Prize Social Issues Dissertation Award from the Society for the Psychological Study of Social Issues ([[Division 9 (SPSSI)]] of the [[American Psychological Association]]).<ref name="uci-faculty" />

Following postdoctoral research at UC Berkeley (2012–2014), Piff joined the faculty at the [[University of California, Irvine]] in 2014 and was promoted to Associate Professor in 2020.<ref name="uci-faculty" /> He has also served in leadership roles associated with UCI’s poverty and inequality research initiatives.<ref name="blum-center">{{cite web |title=People |url=https://blumcenter.uci.edu/people/ |website=Blum Center for Poverty Alleviation |publisher=University of California, Irvine |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2025-08-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250819090010/https://blumcenter.uci.edu/people/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

== Research ==

Piff's work focuses on three areas: [[social class]] and ethics, the psychology of awe, and theoretical accounts of social class as a cultural system.<ref name="greater-good" />

=== Social class and ethics ===

In a 2010 paper published in the ''[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]'', Piff and colleagues reported that individuals from lower socioeconomic backgrounds exhibited higher levels of generosity and prosocial behavior than those from wealthier backgrounds.<ref name="having-less">{{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Kraus |first2=Michael W. |last3=Côté |first3=Stéphane |last4=Cheng |first4=Bonnie Hayden |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |title=Having less, giving more: The influence of social class on prosocial behavior |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |year=2010 |volume=99 |issue=5 |pages=771–784 |doi=10.1037/a0020092 |pmid=20649364 |url=https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20649364/ |archive-date=2025-11-18 |access-date=2026-02-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251118064505/https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20649364/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

His most widely discussed work, published in 2012 in the [[Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences]], is titled “Higher social class predicts increased unethical behavior.” Across seven studies, the paper reported that individuals from higher social classes were more likely to break traffic laws, engage in unethical decision-making in hypothetical scenarios, take valued goods from others, lie in negotiations, and endorse unethical behavior at work. The research suggested that favorable attitudes toward greed may contribute to these differences.<ref name="pnas-2012">{{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Stancato |first2=Daniel M. |last3=Côté |first3=Stéphane |last4=Mendoza-Denton |first4=Rodolfo |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |title=Higher social class predicts increased unethical behavior |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |year=2012 |volume=109 |issue=11 |pages=4086–4091 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1118373109 |url=https://www.pnas.org/doi/abs/10.1073/pnas.1118373109 |archive-date=2025-08-10 |access-date=2026-02-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250810155358/https://www.pnas.org/doi/abs/10.1073/pnas.1118373109 |url-status=live }}</ref> The paper received widespread media coverage<ref name="berkeley-news">{{cite news |title=Upper class more likely to be scofflaws due to greed, study finds |url=https://news.berkeley.edu/2012/02/27/greed/ |work=Berkeley News |date=February 27, 2012 |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2025-05-16 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250516120306/http://news.berkeley.edu/2012/02/27/greed/ |url-status=live }}</ref> and has been widely cited in the academic literature.<ref name="scholar">{{cite web |title=Paul K Piff - Google Scholar |url=https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=0jIzeRAAAAAJ&hl=en |website=Google Scholar |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2024-06-06 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240606135744/https://scholar.google.com/citations?hl=en&user=0jIzeRAAAAAJ |url-status=live }}</ref>

The PNAS paper also attracted scholarly criticism and subsequent replication efforts. Statistician Greg Francis published a response arguing that the probability of all seven studies reaching [[statistical significance]] was unusually low, suggesting possible [[publication bias]].<ref name="francis">{{cite journal |last=Francis |first=Gregory |title=Evidence that publication bias contaminated studies relating social class and unethical behavior |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |year=2012 |volume=109 |issue=25 |pages=E1587 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1203591109 |url=https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3382534/}}</ref> Piff and his team published a reply in the same journal, critiquing Francis’s statistical approach; others have raised similar concerns.<ref name="piff-response">{{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Stancato |first2=Daniel M. |last3=Côté |first3=Stéphane |last4=Mendoza-Denton |first4=Rodolfo |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |title=Reply to Francis: Methods, measures, and explanations |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |year=2012 |volume=109 |issue=25 |pages=E1588 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1205367109 |url=https://www.pnas.org/doi/abs/10.1073/pnas.1205367109}}</ref><ref name="simonsohn-2012">{{cite journal |last=Simonsohn |first=Uri |title=It Does Not Follow: Evaluating the Concept of 'Test Excess Success' |journal=Perspectives on Psychological Science |year=2012 |volume=7 |issue=6 |pages=597–599 |doi=10.1177/1745691612463399 |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/1745691612463399 |access-date=2026-03-09 |url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name="simonsohn-2013">{{cite journal |last=Simonsohn |first=Uri |title=Just Post It: The Lesson From Two Cases of Fabricated Data Detected by Statistics Alone |journal=Journal of Mathematical Psychology |year=2013 |volume=57 |issue=1 |pages=1–2 |doi=10.1016/j.jmp.2013.03.001 |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S002224961300031X |access-date=2026-03-09 |url-access=subscription }}</ref> In 2017, researchers at the University of Western Ontario identified a mathematical error in the original paper; Piff’s team acknowledged it as minor and not affecting the overall conclusions, and in turn noted an inversion error in the replication team’s analysis. Corrections were issued, described by ''[[Retraction Watch]]'' as a collaborative exchange.<ref name="retraction-watch">{{cite news |title=Are rich people meaner? Trying to find out, two teams find errors in each other's work |url=https://retractionwatch.com/2017/10/13/rich-people-meaner-trying-find-two-teams-find-errors-others-work/ |work=Retraction Watch |date=October 13, 2017 |access-date=2026-02-17}}</ref><ref name="pnas-correction">{{cite journal |title=Correction for Piff et al. |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |year=2017 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1716910114 |url=https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1716910114 |archive-date=2025-08-10 |access-date=2026-02-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250810155042/https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.1716910114 |url-status=live |pmc=5664563 }}</ref> Regarding the original field studies on driver behavior, Coughenour and colleagues replicated the patterns, finding that drivers of higher-cost cars were less likely to yield for pedestrians.<ref name="coughenour">{{cite journal |last1=Coughenour |first1=Courtney |title=Examining the effects of driver status on pedestrian yielding |journal=Journal of Transport & Health |year=2020 |doi=10.1016/j.jth.2020.100831 |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2214140520300359 |archive-date=2024-10-07 |access-date=2026-02-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241007155559/https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S2214140520300359 |url-status=live |pmc=8758047 }}</ref> However, Jung et al. (2023) published two preregistered direct replications and found no evidence of a relationship between vehicle status and unethical driving behavior.<ref name="jung-2023">{{cite journal |last1=Jung |first1=Minah H. |title=Social status and unethical behavior: Two replications of the field studies in Piff et al. (2012) |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General |year=2023 |pmid=36603114 |url=https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36603114/ |archive-date=2025-11-15 |access-date=2026-02-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251115183320/https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36603114/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

In subsequent research, Piff reported in 2014 that wealthier individuals scored higher on measures of [[narcissism]] and entitlement, and that experimentally inducing feelings of egalitarianism was associated with reductions in these tendencies.<ref name="narcissism">{{cite journal |last=Piff |first=Paul K. |title=Wealth and the inflated self: Class, entitlement, and narcissism |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |year=2014 |volume=40 |issue=1 |pages=34–43 |doi=10.1177/0146167213501699 |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0146167213501699 |archive-date=2025-05-07 |access-date=2026-02-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250507072241/https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/0146167213501699 |url-status=live |url-access=subscription }}</ref> A 2020 study published in [[Nature Human Behaviour]] found that shifting people's explanations for [[poverty]] from individual failings to situational and structural factors increased their opposition to inequality and support for egalitarian policies.<ref name="nature-2020">{{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Wiwad |first2=Dylan |last3=Robinson |first3=Angela R. |last4=Aknin |first4=Lara B. |last5=Mercier |first5=Brett |last6=Shariff |first6=Azim |title=Shifting attributions for poverty motivates opposition to inequality and enhances egalitarianism |journal=Nature Human Behaviour |year=2020 |volume=4 |pages=496–505 |doi=10.1038/s41562-020-0835-8 |url=https://www.nature.com/articles/s41562-020-0835-8 |archive-date=2024-06-22 |access-date=2026-02-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240622025803/https://www.nature.com/articles/s41562-020-0835-8 |url-status=live |url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Awe and prosocial behavior ===

Piff's second major research area examines the psychological and social effects of [[awe]]. In a 2015 paper published in the ''[[Journal of Personality and Social Psychology]]'', Piff and colleagues presented five studies demonstrating that experiences of awe diminish the emphasis on the individual self, what the authors termed "the small self" effect,  which was associated with [[prosocial behavior]] such as generosity, helping, and ethical decision-making. In one field experiment, participants who spent a minute looking up at a grove of towering [[eucalyptus]] trees subsequently helped a researcher pick up more dropped pens than participants who looked up at a tall building.<ref name="awe-2015">{{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Dietze |first2=Pia |last3=Feinberg |first3=Matthew |last4=Stancato |first4=Daniel M. |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |title=Awe, the small self, and prosocial behavior |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |year=2015 |volume=108 |issue=6 |pages=883–899 |doi=10.1037/pspi0000018}}</ref>

Subsequent research has extended these findings to large-scale natural events. A 2022 study published in ''[[Psychological Science]]'' examined the 2017 total solar eclipse and found that communities in the path of [[Solar eclipse|totality]] showed increases in prosocial behavior and social cohesion compared with communities that experienced only a partial eclipse.<ref name="eclipse">{{cite journal |last1=Goldy |first1=Sean P. |last2=Jones |first2=Nickolas M. |last3=Piff |first3=Paul K. |title=The social effects of an awesome solar eclipse |journal=Psychological Science |year=2022 |volume=33 |issue=9 |pages=1452–1462 |doi=10.1177/09567976221085501|doi-access=free }}</ref> Piff has also published research on how engagement with nature, particularly awe-inspiring natural environments such as [[Sequoiadendron giganteum|giant sequoia]] groves, is associated with social connection and well-being, with support from the [[United States Forest Service]].<ref name="uci-faculty" />

=== Social class as culture ===

Together with [[Michael W. Kraus]] and Dacher Keltner, Piff has contributed to a theoretical framework that promotes social class as a distinct cultural system. Their 2011 paper in ''[[Current Directions in Psychological Science]]'' argued that social class shapes values, cognitive styles, and emotional patterns in ways comparable to ethnic or national cultures, with lower-class environments fostering more [[collectivism|collectivist]] orientations and upper-class environments fostering more [[individualism|individualist]] ones.<ref name="class-culture">{{cite journal |last1=Kraus |first1=Michael W. |last2=Piff |first2=Paul K. |last3=Keltner |first3=Dacher |title=Social class as culture: The convergence of resources and rank in the social realm |journal=Current Directions in Psychological Science |year=2011 |volume=20 |issue=4 |pages=246–250 |doi=10.1177/0963721411414654 |url=https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0963721411414654 |archive-date=2025-08-10 |access-date=2026-02-27 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250810155039/https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0963721411414654 |url-status=live |url-access=subscription }}</ref> A more comprehensive review of this framework was published in 2012 in ''[[Psychological Review]]''.<ref name="psych-review">{{cite journal |last1=Kraus |first1=Michael W. |last2=Piff |first2=Paul K. |last3=Mendoza-Denton |first3=Rodolfo |last4=Rheinschmidt |first4=Michelle L. |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |title=Social class, solipsism, and contextualism: How the rich are different from the poor |journal=Psychological Review |year=2012 |volume=119 |issue=3 |pages=546–572 |doi=10.1037/a0028756}}</ref>

== Media and public engagement ==

=== TEDx talks ===

In 2013, Piff delivered a TEDx Marin talk titled “Does Money Make You Mean?”, in which he described a rigged [[Monopoly (game)|Monopoly]] game experiment illustrating the effects of structural advantages on behavior.<ref name="ted-talk" /> Participants given advantages exhibited more dominant behavior, consumed more from a shared bowl of snacks, and tended to attribute their success to personal strategy rather than structural factors.<ref name="ted-talk" /><ref name="npr">{{cite news |title=Does Money Make You Mean? |url=https://www.npr.org/transcripts/295360962 |work=NPR TED Radio Hour |date=April 4, 2014 |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2025-08-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250802032330/https://www.npr.org/transcripts/295360962 |url-status=live }}</ref> In September 2022, he returned to TEDx Marin with a talk on “The Science of Awe,” reflecting his research on the social functions of awe.<ref name="tedx-2022">{{cite web |title=TEDxMarin September 2022 Speakers |url=https://tedxmarin.org/september-2022/ |website=TEDxMarin |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2025-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251207035421/https://tedxmarin.org/september-2022/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Media coverage ===

Piff has been profiled in New York magazine and his research has been covered in ''[[The New York Times]]'',<ref name="nyt-awe">{{cite news |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Keltner |first2=Dacher |title=Why Do We Experience Awe? |work=The New York Times |date=May 22, 2015 |url=https://www.psychologicalscience.org/news/why-do-we-experience-awe.html |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2025-03-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250322150834/https://www.psychologicalscience.org/news/why-do-we-experience-awe.html |url-status=live }}</ref> ''[[Scientific American]]'',<ref name="sci-am">{{cite news |last=Grewal |first=Daisy |title=How Wealth Reduces Compassion |url=https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/how-wealth-reduces-compassion/ |work=Scientific American |date=April 10, 2012 |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2026-02-05 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260205113457/https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/how-wealth-reduces-compassion/ |url-status=live }}</ref> [[NPR]],<ref name="npr" /> [[PBS NewsHour]],<ref name="pbs-narcissism">{{cite news |title=Does Wealth Breed Narcissism? |url=https://www.pbs.org/newshour/economy/does-wealth-breed-narcissism-the-new-mirror-mirror-on-the-wall-study |work=PBS NewsHour |access-date=2026-02-17}}</ref> [[CBC Radio]],<ref name="cbc">{{cite news |title=Are the Rich Less Feeling? |url=https://www.cbc.ca/radio/thecurrent/oct-16-2013-1.2909261/are-the-rich-less-feeling-research-suggests-wealth-reduces-compassion-1.2909264 |work=CBC Radio - The Current |date=October 16, 2013 |access-date=2026-02-17}}</ref> and the [[World Economic Forum]].<ref name="wef">{{cite news |last1=Robinson |first1=Angela R. |last2=Piff |first2=Paul K. |title=Wealth can make us selfish and stingy. Two psychologists explain why |url=https://www.weforum.org/stories/2016/10/wealth-can-make-us-selfish-and-stingy-two-psychologists-explain-why/ |work=World Economic Forum |date=October 5, 2016 |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2026-02-01 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260201002254/https://www.weforum.org/stories/2016/10/wealth-can-make-us-selfish-and-stingy-two-psychologists-explain-why/ |url-status=live }}</ref> In 2012, [[New York (magazine)|''New York'' ''magazine'']] published a cover story by Lisa Miller titled "The Money-Empathy Gap," profiling Piff as "one of a new generation of scientists...who are exploiting this moment of unprecedented income inequality to explore behaviors."<ref name="nymag" /> He has co-authored op-eds for ''The New York Times'' with Dacher Keltner and contributed pieces to [[PBS]] and the ''[[Los Angeles Times]]''.<ref name="pbs-piece">{{cite news |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Robinson |first2=Angela R. |title=You Didn't Build That: Why Remembering Your Good Fortune Can Be Good for You and Others |url=https://www.pbs.org/wnet/chasing-the-dream/2017/05/didnt-build-remembering-good-fortune-can-good-others/ |work=PBS Chasing the Dream |date=May 11, 2017 |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2024-01-10 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240110002450/https://www.pbs.org/wnet/chasing-the-dream/2017/05/didnt-build-remembering-good-fortune-can-good-others/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Documentary appearances ===

Piff has appeared as an expert in several documentary films:

* ''[[Park Avenue: Money, Power and the American Dream]]'' (2012), directed by [[Alex Gibney]], which aired on [[PBS]] ''[[Independent Lens]]'' and examines the wealth gap between Park Avenue's billionaires and the [[South Bronx]].<ref name="park-ave">{{cite web |title=Park Avenue: Money, Power and the American Dream |url=https://www.pbs.org/independentlens/documentaries/park-avenue/ |website=PBS Independent Lens |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2017-07-20 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170720203933/http://www.pbs.org/independentlens/videos/park-avenue-money-power-the-american-dream-2/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
* ''[[Capital in the Twenty-First Century (film)|Capital in the Twenty-First Century]]'' (2019), directed by Justin Pemberton and based on [[Thomas Piketty]]'s bestselling book, in which Piff discusses the rigged Monopoly experiment alongside economists and public intellectuals.<ref name="capital-imdb">{{cite web |title=Capital in the Twenty-First Century |url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt5723056/ |website=IMDb |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2026-02-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260219183116/https://www.imdb.com/title/tt5723056/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
* ''12th Hour'' (2021), directed by Susan Kucera, a documentary examining humanity's capacity to respond to [[climate change]].<ref name="12th-hour">{{cite web |title=12th Hour |url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt15475336/ |website=IMDb |access-date=2026-02-17}}</ref>
* ''Giants Rising'' (2024), written and directed by Lisa Landers, in which Piff discusses the impacts of experiencing awe.<ref name="giants-rising">{{cite web |title=Giants Rising |url=http://giantsrising.com/ |website=Giants Rising |access-date=2026-02-26 |archive-date=2025-07-16 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250716032013/https://www.giantsrising.com/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

== Awards and honors ==

* 2020 – Dean's Award for Inclusionary Excellence, School of Social Ecology, UC Irvine<ref name="uci-faculty" />
* 2019 – Sage Young Scholar Award, [[Society for Personality and Social Psychology]]<ref name="spsp-sage">{{cite web |title=SAGE Young Scholars Recipients |url=https://spsp.org/news-center/news/sage-young-scholars-recipients |website=Society for Personality and Social Psychology |access-date=2026-02-17}}</ref>
* 2017 – Fellow, [[Society of Experimental Social Psychology]]<ref name="uci-faculty" />
* 2016 – Rising Star, [[Association for Psychological Science]]<ref name="aps-rising">{{cite web |title=Paul Piff Profile |url=https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/paul_piff |website=Greater Good Science Center |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2026-01-19 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260119170824/https://greatergood.berkeley.edu/profile/paul_piff |url-status=live }}</ref>
* 2015 – Emerging Leadership in Psychology, [[American Psychological Association]] Committee on Socioeconomic Status<ref name="apa-cses">{{cite web |title=CSES Leadership Awards |url=https://www.apa.org/about/awards/cses-leadership-award?tab=3 |website=American Psychological Association |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2021-06-30 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210630014057/https://www.apa.org/about/awards/cses-leadership-award?tab=3 |url-status=live }}</ref>
* 2012 – First Prize, Social Issues Dissertation Award, [[Society for the Psychological Study of Social Issues]] (Division 9 of the APA)<ref name="diss-award">{{cite web |title=Social Issues Dissertation Award |url=https://www.apa.org/about/awards/div-9-socissdis |website=American Psychological Association |access-date=2026-02-17 |archive-date=2025-12-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251212075126/https://www.apa.org/about/awards/div-9-socissdis |url-status=live }}</ref>
* 2008–2011 – [[National Science Foundation]] Graduate Research Fellowship<ref name="uci-faculty" />
* 2004 – [[Phi Beta Kappa]], [[Reed College]]<ref name="uci-faculty" />

== Selected publications ==

* {{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Kraus |first2=Michael W. |last3=Côté |first3=Stéphane |last4=Cheng |first4=Bonnie Hayden |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |title=Having less, giving more: The influence of social class on prosocial behavior |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |year=2010 |volume=99 |issue=5 |pages=771–784 |doi=10.1037/a0020092 |pmid=20649364}}
* {{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Stancato |first2=Daniel M. |last3=Côté |first3=Stéphane |last4=Mendoza-Denton |first4=Rodolfo |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |title=Higher social class predicts increased unethical behavior |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |year=2012 |volume=109 |issue=11 |pages=4086–4091 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1118373109}}
* {{cite journal |last1=Kraus |first1=Michael W. |last2=Piff |first2=Paul K. |last3=Mendoza-Denton |first3=Rodolfo |last4=Rheinschmidt |first4=Michelle L. |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |title=Social class, solipsism, and contextualism: How the rich are different from the poor |journal=Psychological Review |year=2012 |volume=119 |issue=3 |pages=546–572 |doi=10.1037/a0028756}}
* {{cite journal |last=Piff |first=Paul K. |title=Wealth and the inflated self: Class, entitlement, and narcissism |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |year=2014 |volume=40 |issue=1 |pages=34–43 |doi=10.1177/0146167213501699}}
* {{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Dietze |first2=Pia |last3=Feinberg |first3=Matthew |last4=Stancato |first4=Daniel M. |last5=Keltner |first5=Dacher |title=Awe, the small self, and prosocial behavior |journal=Journal of Personality and Social Psychology |year=2015 |volume=108 |issue=6 |pages=883–899 |doi=10.1037/pspi0000018}}
* {{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Kraus |first2=Michael W. |last3=Keltner |first3=Dacher |title=Unpacking the inequality paradox: Psychological roots of inequality and social class |journal=Advances in Experimental Social Psychology |year=2018 |volume=57 |pages=53–124}}
* {{cite journal |last1=Piff |first1=Paul K. |last2=Wiwad |first2=Dylan |last3=Robinson |first3=Angela R. |last4=Aknin |first4=Lara B. |last5=Mercier |first5=Brett |last6=Shariff |first6=Azim |title=Shifting attributions for poverty motivates opposition to inequality and enhances egalitarianism |journal=Nature Human Behaviour |year=2020 |volume=4 |pages=496–505 |doi=10.1038/s41562-020-0835-8}}
* {{cite journal |last1=Goldy |first1=Sean P. |last2=Jones |first2=Nickolas M. |last3=Piff |first3=Paul K. |title=The social effects of an awesome solar eclipse |journal=Psychological Science |year=2022 |volume=33 |issue=9 |pages=1452–1462 |doi=10.1177/09567976221085501|doi-access=free }}
